summary: In this TEDxBerkeley talk, Fernando Pérez shares his personal journey from growing up in violence-torn Medellín, Colombia to building IPython and Jupyter, tools that transformed scientific computing. He describes how getting fired by his PhD advisor inadvertently led him to create IPython as a conversational computing tool, which grew into the Jupyter project through open community collaboration. Pérez emphasizes that the open source model enabled unprecedented scientific achievements, including the LIGO gravitational wave discovery, and revolutionized education at UC Berkeley through the Foundations of Data Science course. He argues that open, collaborative development is humanity's best chance at ensuring the data revolution benefits society broadly rather than enriching only a few.
key_points: IPython began as a personal tool for conversational computing during grad school and evolved into the Jupyter project through community effort, not solo heroism
Open source scientific tools enabled breakthrough science like the LIGO gravitational wave detection, vindicating years of work dismissed as career waste
The open model empowers global participation — students from impoverished rural India could contribute to the same research tools used at top universities
Data science education at Berkeley grew into the fastest-growing courses in the university's history, built entirely on openly developed technologies
The data revolution demands open collaboration to ensure benefits reach society broadly rather than driving further inequality
money_quotes: I told her it would take about an afternoon. It's been about 18 years I've been working on this.

In this space of open source scientific development, what we found was like-minded people who were interested in building together rather than proving who's the smartest person in the room and destroying each other's work.
It is important to go against this narrative of solo heroes in science, which is something the media likes to use a lot but is unfortunately very toxic and very counterproductive.

We had been building these tools, being told that it was a waste of our time, of our career, that it wasn't considered real work. And here we are: scientists at the cutting edge of physics using tools constructed entirely with the open source software that we've been building.

I believe the best shot we have at ensuring that those changes are positive changes for as much of society as we can is to actually continue on this kind of journey of building things openly and collaboratively.
That little whoop at the end — that is the sound of two black holes merging into one another, 1.3 billion light years away, at the end of the universe.
None of this was done because we were given a mandate. It was done because people rolled up their sleeves and decided they wanted to do it.
key_insight: Open, community-driven tools built without mandate or institutional support became the foundation for both frontier science and transformative education, proving that collaborative generosity outperforms proprietary gatekeeping.
